mappings.rationaleAI: The world's highest-volume grain crop, domesticated 9,000 years ago in Mexico, corresponds to cereals as a major crop (C.6.b).
Legume-family seeds cultivated as vegetables and protein sources correspond to vegetables and legumes as horticultural crops (C.6.a).
Edible plant parts including roots, stems, leaves, and seeds correspond to vegetables and legumes (C.6.a) and fruit and vegetable processing (F.1).
Animal tissue consumed as food from domesticated cattle, pigs, sheep, and chickens corresponds to meat and meat products processing (F.5).

Nutrient-rich liquid from mammalian mammary glands processed via pasteurization and homogenization corresponds to dairying and dairy products (F.7) and milk production and handling techniques (F.7.a).
Staple food for over half the global population, domesticated in China 13,500-8,200 years ago, corresponds to cereals (C.6.b).
Agricultural management of livestock corresponds to livestock farming (D), animal breeding (D.1), major flock and stock animals (D.2), and disease and pest control (D.3).
The world's leading source of vegetable protein and a globally dominant cereal crop corresponds to cereals (C.6.b).
Dairy product produced by coagulating milk protein corresponds to dairy products including butter and cheese (F.7.b).
Beverages including coffee, tea, beer, wine, and spirits correspond to beverage production (F.8).
Catching fish and other aquatic animals from natural water bodies corresponds to technology of hunting and fishing (E).
Domesticated ruminants raised for wool, meat, and milk correspond to sheep and goats as major livestock (D.2.c).
Comprehensive coverage of cultivation and livestock production corresponds to the history of agriculture (A), farm management (B), crop farming (C), and livestock farming (D).
Chocolate derived from fermented, dried, roasted cacao seeds corresponds to cocoa and chocolate products (F.13).
Seeds, fruits, roots, and bark used for flavoring and preserving food correspond to spices, herbs, and flavourings (F.9).
Beverage produced from Camellia sinensis leaves corresponds to technology of producing nonalcoholic beverages including tea (F.8.d).
Substances consumed for nutritional support broadly correspond to food processing (F), food preservation (G), techniques for controlling food quality (H), and new product development (I).
Baked staple made from flour, water, and leavening agents corresponds to bakery products (F.3).
Multi-generational process controlling species for food and labor corresponds to the history of agriculture (A) and animal breeding (D.1).
Genetic engineering and tissue culture for GMO crops correspond to control of pests and disease organisms (C.5) and development of new food sources (I).
Developed pasteurization technique for milk and wine corresponding to high-temperature preservation including pasteurization (G.1.c) and milk production and handling techniques (F.7.a).
Grasses cultivated for edible grains correspond to cereals as major crops (C.6.b) and cereals, cereal products, and starch products processing (F.2).
Pursuing and killing wildlife for food and management corresponds to technology of hunting and fishing (E).
The world's most numerous domesticated animal with over 50 billion produced annually corresponds to poultry as major livestock (D.2.e).
Large bovid ungulates raised for meat, dairy, leather, and draft power correspond to cattle as major livestock (D.2.a).
Horses domesticated for warfare, agriculture, and transport correspond to horses as major livestock (D.2.d).
Plant nutrients supplying nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium to soil correspond to soil preparation and care (C.1).
Spans Neolithic origins through the Green Revolution, directly corresponding to the history of agriculture (A).
Applying heat to food to improve palatability, digestibility, and safety connects broadly to food processing (F).
Seed-bearing structures of flowering plants correspond to fruits and nuts as horticultural crops (C.6.a) and fruit processing (F.1).
Enclosed heating chambers for cooking relate to bakery product production methods (F.3).
Starchy tuberous vegetable domesticated in the Andes corresponds to vegetables as horticultural crops (C.6.a).
Environmental contaminants from agriculture correspond to farming in relation to pollution control (B.4).
Biochemical processes using food to sustain life connect to techniques for controlling the quality of food (H).
Aquatic vertebrates as food source correspond to technology of fishing (E) and fish and marine products processing (F.6).
Eggs as reproductive vessels consumed as food correspond to eggs and egg products (F.12).
Controlled application of water to sustain crops corresponds to soil preparation and care (C.1).
Engines powering farm machinery correspond to farm labour, draft animals, and farm machinery (B.2).
ICEs powering tractors and farm machinery correspond to farm machinery (B.2).
The foundational transition from hunter-gatherer subsistence to sedentary agriculture corresponds to the history of agriculture (A).
Oldest food seasoning and preservative corresponds to chemical preservation (G.1.e) and spices, herbs, and flavourings (F.9).
Sucrose extracted from sugarcane and sugar beet corresponds to cane sugar, beet sugar, and other sweeteners (F.10).
Ethanol-containing drinks produced by fermentation and distillation correspond to technology of brewing (F.8.a), wine making (F.8.b), and producing distilled liquor (F.8.c).
Caffeinated beverage from Coffea plant corresponds to coffee as a major field crop (C.6.d) and technology of producing nonalcoholic beverages (F.8.d).
Domesticated omnivore producing approximately 120 million tonnes of pork annually corresponds to swine as major livestock (D.2.b).
Perennial woody plants correspond to forest crops including trees (C.6.c).
Dynamic mixture of minerals, organic matter, and water functioning as a plant-growth medium corresponds to soil preparation and care (C.1).
Temperature maintenance below ambient for food preservation corresponds to low-temperature preservation including refrigeration and freezing (G.1.a).
Machines used in agricultural operations correspond to farm machinery (B.2).
